title: Penn State notebook | Softball coach Clarisa Crowell talks Oregon series, upcoming home opener
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State had a tough outing in its opening weekend of Big Ten play, going 0-3 against No.17 Oregon. This marked the end of the Nittany Lions' six weeks on the road, as they play host for the first time of the season this weekend, welcoming Wisconsin.

In Wednesday's media session, coach Clarisa Crowell discussed the blue and white’s effort so far this season and the positive energy the team is taking into its return to Beard Field.

Winless in Eugene

Penn State faced both elite pitching and offense in its opening conference series. The Nittany Lions fell by overwhelming margins in their first two games, but challenged the Ducks in Game 3, eventually falling in walkoff fashion.

“I think when you're facing an elite-level pitcher, and actually, they just had great pitching all around and great offense, you're forced to find ways to grow as a team and get one percent better,” Crowell said. “I think that experience this past weekend certainly just showed us that we still got a lot of room to grow.”

In the three games combined, the blue and white were out-hit 29-8. The difference in the box score from the first two games to the last was still evident, though.

Penn State combined for just three runs in the first two games, compared to Oregon’s 18. However, in the series finale, the Nittany Lions managed five runs, losing by one, and the pitching allowed the lowest amount of hits it had all weekend.

“Anytime you could put yourself in a situation to win a game three, especially how that series went, I think it says a lot about your team,” Crowell said. “I was really proud of how our kids played and how well they fought that game.”

Return to Beard

For the first time since May 4 of last year, Penn State is set to play on its home dirt. The Nittany Lions were previously scheduled to host an invitational the first week of March, but it was canceled due to weather.

The blue and white have been traveling every weekend since the season started on Feb. 6.

“Our home tournament got canceled, so that's why I think it felt a little bit longer for them,” Crowell said. “This is even better, because now they're even more excited, because we're in Big Ten play.”

Home-field advantage is real, especially in a game like softball when it's crucial to know the makeup of a diamond. Penn State has lost just five games at home in each of its last two seasons.

It’s also a plus to be able to play in front of your fans and bring positive energy.

“Anytime you can play at home it's awesome, especially this is our first weekend having an opportunity to play in front of our home crowd,” Crowell said. “I think just a lot of positive vibes that's going to be happening, we're excited, just hoping that the weather holds up for us this weekend, but it's gonna be a great weekend.”

Pitching as a backbone

Last season, the Nittany Lions found almost all their success in the batter's box, and struggled to find consistency in the circle.

This year is as different as Crowell, with five pitchers that she can regularly rely on, and they’re going to be the key to the blue and white’s success in Big Ten play.

Penn State’s pitching had been pretty much on point this season, but against a high-octane Oregon team, the staff had its struggles.

“This weekend, honestly, when I look at it, maybe it was good for our pitching staff, because we really haven't had this happen,” Crowell said. “We've had so much success in the circle this year that this is a good eye opener for our staff and of the importance of starting fast.”

Starting fast and setting the tone of the game in the circle is what the Nittany Lions have a game plan to do against a Wisconsin team batting .343.

“It's going to be a series where the pitching is going to have to hold them down,” Crowell said. “But we're going to have to out-hit them, and I think we can do that.”

Six Penn State skaters were honored with Big Ten postseason accolades on Wednesday.

title: Air Force hires Penn State men's basketball assistant Joe Crispin as next head coach
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is set to have some turnover on the coaching staff.

Air Force has hired assistant coach Joe Crispin as its next head coach, the Falcons announced Wednesday after college basketball insider Jon Rothstein reported the news first.

Sources: Air Force will hire Penn State's Joe Crispin as its next head basketball coach.— Jon Rothstein (@JonRothstein) March 18, 2026

Crispin has been on the Nittany Lions’ staff since the 2023-24 season. He began as the assistant to the head coach before changing titles to assistant coach ahead of this season.

During his time on staff, Crispin helped with Penn State’s offense. He guided the Nittany Lions to set a program record in the 2024-25 season with an average of 79.1 points per game.

Crispin played at Penn State from 1997-01, and he led the team to a Sweet 16 appearance in the 2001 NCAA Tournament. He averaged 15.6 points per game across 127 appearances in his collegiate career.

Prior to rejoining his alma mater, Crispin was the head coach at Rowan University from 2016-23.

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State baseball continues struggled play, pitching woes continue
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
The rocky season continued for Penn State as the Nittany Lions dropped four of their five games last week.

Before traveling back to Happy Valley for its first Big Ten series of the campaign, the blue and white lost two games against Georgetown. Despite the Nittany Lions winning the series opener against Iowa in convincing fashion, the Hawkeyes won the other two games in blowout fashion.

Now in the midst of a tough conference schedule, Penn State has many areas to improve upon and turn its 5-13 season around.

Pitching problems persist

The Nittany Lions’ pitchers have delivered subpar performances so far this season. With a team ERA of 8.49, the blue and white hold the worst ERA in the Big Ten. Additionally, Penn State has surrendered the third-most walks (101), the most hits (198) and earned runs (139) and tallied the second-least strikeouts (127).

In the game, one 8-2 loss against the Hoyas, redshirt senior Frankie Sanchez didn’t last long in his outing. The right-hander surrendered three hits, three walks and four earned runs over just 2.2 innings of work.

The blue and white’s close 5-4 loss the next day offered slightly different results. Freshman Ethan Bauerschmidt delivered a quality start on the mound, allowing just three earned runs, five hits and one walk over four innings. The bullpen allowed just one more earned run the rest of the game, with Georgetown scoring the walkoff run in the eighth.

Despite the rough pitching performances throughout the week, redshirt senior Colin Fitzgerald threw a great game against one of the best offenses in the Big Ten. In Penn State’s lone 11-6 victory of the week, the right-hander held the Hawkeyes scoreless through seven frames and surrendered just three earned runs, four hits and totaled seven strikeouts.

Yet, Iowa jumped on sophomore starter Ben Hudson early in Saturday’s 13-4 loss. The Medford, New Jersey, native surrendered seven earned runs and 12 hits over 4.2 innings. In a 7-1 deficit entering the bottom of the sixth, the blue and white’s offense couldn’t mount a comeback.

Freshman Isaiah Shayter got the ball for the Nittany Lions in their 12-4 series finale loss. Despite entering the fourth frame up 3-0, Iowa plated seven runs in the inning and ran away with the game.

Shayter threw 3.1 innings and allowed four earned runs, four hits and three walks. While it wasn’t a strong start, the bullpen didn’t provide much relief. Junior Kyle Emmons surrendered four hits and four earned runs over three innings, so Penn State’s offense couldn’t close the distance.

The bright spots

Though the blue and white’s offense has been inconsistent, there were some players who provided offensive sparks throughout these five games.

Senior Michael Anderson tallied three multi-hit games throughout the week. The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native homered against Georgetown and Iowa, lifting his total to a team-best eight home runs. The Arkansas transfer is an essential bat in coach Mike Gambino’s lineup, so his steady production will be needed throughout the campaign.

Additionally, redshirt junior Bryce Molinaro recorded at least one hit in each game until the series finale against Iowa. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native homered in both losses against the Hoyas and had two hits in Friday’s loss to Iowa.

However, the Nittany Lions need consistent production outside of these two power hitters. While there weren't many positives after just one win in this slate of games, Penn State still has plenty of time to turn the season around.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field’s rising stars ahead of 2026 outdoor season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Just one week after the NCAA indoor championships, Penn State is back in action, taking the track with the Penn Challenge on Saturday.

The Nittany Lions had several athletes experience breakout seasons during the recent indoor season, and have many more on the way this spring.

Here’s a look at who could be among the rising stars for Penn State this outdoor season, kicking off on Saturday.

Addyson White

In just her second year with the blue and white, Addyson White has emerged as the team’s leading sprinter on the women’s side and has made improvements each season.

Not only has she been the go-to 60-meter runner and one of the best 200-meter sprinters on the team, but White also leads the Nittany Lions in the 60m hurdles.

After not being selected to compete in the 2025 Big Ten championships, White finished 23rd this year in the 60m as she cut 0.14 seconds off last year’s personal best.

Carter Fitzgerald

While he only has one season left with the blue and white, senior Carter Fitzgerald is slated for his best outdoor season yet after a record-breaking indoor campaign.

Fitzgerald ran just one race during the 2026 indoor season, setting the No. 2 mark in school history in the 500-meter with a time of 1:01.97. The Doylestown, Pennsylvania, native then finished 12th in the 600-meter at the Big Ten championship.

Boh Ritchie

After graduating high school in December, New Zealand native Boh Ritchie took the track by storm during her first season, setting the second-best mark in school history in a pair of events.

While Ritchie already achieved greatness in just 10 weeks of collegiate track and field, she’s bound for much more as she becomes even more acclimated to the high level of the Big Ten and NCAA.

In her first two meets of the year, she set the No. 2 time in the 600m with a time of 1:28.21, followed by a 2:01.60 for the No. 2 time in the 800m. Ritchie went on to earn a fourth-place finish in the Big Ten 800m, followed by an 11th-place finish at the NCAA championship.

Niko Schultz

Following three years at Nebraska, senior Niko Schultz ran a 1:48.14 in his first 800m with the Nittany Lions. Two weeks later, he ran a personal-best 1:46.53 for the seventh-best time in school history, followed by a third-place finish at the conference meet and Second Team All-American honors at the NCAA championship.

Schultz had a very clear improvement with the blue and white, including the third-best 600m time in school history after winning the prelims at the Big Ten championship.

With one more season practicing with one of the best mid-distance programs in the country, Schultz should see even more growth in his final go-around.

Skye Petrie-Cameron

Freshman Skye Petrie-Cameron has emerged as one of the blue and white’s best female throwers after earning the No. 4 shot put throw in school history.

As a shot put specialist, Petrie-Cameron competed in six meets this season, throwing over 16 meters in two. During the Big Ten championship, the Jamaica Plain, Massachusetts, native set a personal-best 16.45 meters, earning seventh place.

Trip Campbell

In just his second collegiate meet, freshman Trip Campbell earned the best high jump mark of any Nittany Lion this season, jumping 2.15 meters to win the Nittany Lion Challenge.

While the other two Penn State high jumpers failed to jump higher than 2.10 meters in any meet this year, Campbell met that mark in the last five of his six competitions this season, including a third-place finish at the Big Ten championship.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's volleyball middle blocker Maggie Mendelson signs with Atlanta Vibe
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Maggie Mendelson is going pro.

The former Nittany Lion signed with the Atlanta Vibe of the NVL on Tuesday, becoming one of the many players in her class to find a new home after college.

Mendelson spent the last two seasons with Penn State, being a major part of the team's national championship run in 2024.

This season, she joined the Lady Lions women's basketball team and made five starts, averaging two points per game.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Satire | What to do at Penn State in the spring weather
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
The weather is heating up in State College and, with this shift towards spring, there are plenty of fun activities for Penn State students to do. Here are my recommendations.

1. Sledding

Now that HUB lawn is clear of all the dangerous snow and ice, it’s finally safe to go sledding with your friends. Grab your sled (or trashbags) and feel free to slide down.

What’s more fun than navigating grass downhill while the sun beats on your head? Just watch out for the sun-bathers — they’re not jump ramps for your sled.

2. Stealing the Willard Preacher’s Hoodie

Most Penn State students have seen the Willard Preacher, and many recognize him as a fixture of the academic building’s eastern steps. He’s an icon in his red hoodie and jeans. I would even say that all Penn State students recognize this outfit.

But, since it’s getting warmer and warmer, Gary doesn’t need that garb right now. Helping him out by stealing his hoodie would be an amazing team-building exercise for any friend group on campus. Not only that, it would also be a concrete way to show him that the student body understands the Christian principle of loving thy neighbor.

3. Baking Cookies

‘Tis the season for holiday baking.

With the harsh conditions outside, it’s important for you and your friends to stay protected from the elements; maybe an indoor activity is more suitable for you.

There’s nothing more festive for spring than staying inside and baking cookies with your friends. You could make chocolate chip, santa-shaped sugar cookies or, perchance, oatmeal raisin.

But, even though this can be safer than some outdoor activities, it’s important to stay safe in the kitchen, too. The oven can get hot! Wear oven mitts (and maybe sunscreen) to keep your baking sesh fun for everyone involved.

And make sure to taste-test.

4. Climbing Beaver Stadium

However, if you love the outdoors, maybe it’s time to try climbing. There are obviously no mountains nearby, and Penn State’s IM building doesn’t have a single rock climbing wall. So, if climbing is your new hobby, it’s time to venture to new heights, literally.

Since Beaver Stadium is currently under construction, there’s no shortage of scaffolding and equipment on the exterior of the stadium. These structures are there to give climbers extra support, so it’s highly recommended for new climbers.

You’ll be ready for Mount Everest in no time, and I can guarantee that you won’t get in the way of any construction workers. This is your turf.

5. Collecting Pollen

Maybe you struggle with seasonal allergies, and are having a hard time finding an activity this season to celebrate the warmer weather and get outside with your friends.

Collecting pollen might just be the activity for you.

Eating natural, local honey is one of the best ways to combat seasonal allergies, according to my almond mom. The only thing that could make her advice better would be making the honey yourself.

It’s super simple to get into beekeeping. Getting a suit and all of the proper materials is incredibly easy and very accessible to everyone, so you should get even more into your new hobby: collect the pollen yourself. How hard can it be, bees do it?

It definitely won’t make your allergies flare up any worse, either, so make sure to tag me in your Barry B. Benson cosplay.

Happy spring, Penn Staters. I hope you were able to find some delightful activity inspiration in this article. But, if not, maybe your spring activity can be suing me. It should be easier to find a lawyer because most injury-related cases happen in the winter, according to Gordon & Gordon.

Enjoy the weather, have fun and be safe.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Column | How Morocco redefined my study abroad experience
author: Daniela White | The Daily Collegian
description:
For those who have studied abroad in Europe, or plan to, you always have a list of places you want to visit. The list usually entails Paris, London, Dublin, Barcelona and Italy. Different cities with different food, different languages and different architecture. None are quite the same, but they have their similarities.

When I was making my own list of places to visit, identical to the one above, my mom told me I had to go to Morocco. She said the country would be unlike any place I’ve ever been to, filled with a language I knew not a single word in and a culture I’ve never seen firsthand.

She kept saying, “You have to go. You have to go,” over and over again, and so I did. I already saw posts over the years of people I knew going, and wearing the traditional turbans and taking photos in the desert at sunset. But when I got there, it was like a whole other world.

While London was home to pop-culture icons, Paris has great monuments like the Eiffel Tower and Dublin is the best place to go to pubs, Morocco was filled with markets you haggle at, dunes of sand that seem like they go on forever and locals that perform music in the middle of the desert at night.

While I appreciate all the different cultures in European cities, being in Morocco was akin to arriving on a different planet, for me at least. The simple-structured tan and russet buildings looked straight out of a film and when the Adhan, an Islamic call to prayer that occurs five times a day, sounded in the streets, I felt like I was in a movie.

In Paris and Barcelona, you’ll see church after church. But in Morocco, there were countless mosques with men in turbans and women in kaftans pouring out of them. Instead of riding a horse in a buggy like London or Dublin, we had the chance to ride on the backs of real-life camels, walking up and down the dunes of the Sahara Desert.

We drove through mountains that touched the clouds and stopped at historic towns where iconic movies and shows such as “Gladiator” and “Game of Thrones” were filmed. I got my turban wrapped by a local and played the authentic Moroccan drums under the stars.

While I love Europe and think Barcelona is the best home base, I think traveling to Morocco was necessary for my study abroad journey. While I appreciate the clubbing culture and endless streets of cafes, it was nice to escape and have a simpler weekend of relaxation and excursions I couldn’t do anywhere in the United States or Europe.

I can truly say Morocco was my favorite place I’ve been to so far while being abroad. It’s a new feeling to be away from society with no service and watch the sun set along the wide, endless horizon of the desert. I danced around a fire to the beats of Moroccan songs sung in Arabic and sipped from the green tea they made in-house. I got to experience things I never could’ve in Barcelona.

While I grew up in a diverse town and was surrounded by many different cultures, experiencing it all in a different country, a different continent, was something I think everyone should do. It makes you realize how different your life is from someone else's and understand how big the world really is.

I had a preconceived idea of what Morocco would be like before visiting, and the city of Marrakesh blew it out of the water. And while I appreciate and love all of the different cultures and cities in Europe, I do think Morocco is a trip everyone should take once in their life.

MORE ABROAD COLUMNS

title: 'We’re going to do everything we can for our fans' | Penn State women’s hockey talks importance of historic Frozen Four
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Head coach Jeff Kampersal, joined alongside Maddy Christian and Kendall Butze, entered a full media room for an uncommon weekday conference on Monday.

Although out of the ordinary, the meeting is historic as Penn State prepares to make its first appearance in the Frozen Four. In addition to playing in it, the Nittany Lions are set to host the rest of the final rounds of the tournament for only the second time.

To reach this monumental achievement, it had to be a goal worked towards throughout the years.

“I think (ending the season at home) has been in the back of our minds since freshman year,” Kendall Butze said. “We’ve known we’re going to host the 2026 Frozen Four for quite some time now. So, I think trying to get the program to a position where we can actually make that a realistic goal for ourselves is something that we’ve been talking about for a few years now.”

For Butze and the rest of the senior class, the focus is now turned to finishing this long-term achievement before leaving Penn State.

With eight total players reaching the end of their collegiate careers, an idea of “finality” grows as they head into their practices for the week.

“It could almost be like a starting pitcher in baseball, where next year’s team will be a brand new team,” Kampersal said. “We lose over a third of our players. Randomly, last Monday’s practice never felt like ‘oh my gosh, this could be our last Monday practice’ and this week there is finality to it.”

Before Kampersal filled the role of head coach for Penn State, the program had accomplished only one winning season. Since then, the reputation has grown over the years, culminating in this historic season.

However, Kampersal gives all the credit to his players and believes the steady upward trend the program is on is due to the efforts of his players.

“These women are dedicated. Dedicated in the weight room and dedicated on the ice,” Kampersal said. “When we got here, there was maybe one player on open ice. Now, players ask the coaches to go out and skate on their own, and that’s just a huge difference. There’s way more coaching and way more involvement in those first years. Now, we guide, but do little. The players do all the work, they let us know when they’re tired and they let us know when we need to push them harder.”

With adding history to an already historic season, consistent awareness around the team grows, filling more seats at Pegula Ice Arena than normal.

A heightened significance and importance weigh on the shoulders of the Nittany Lions as they look to win the first national championship in Penn State hockey history. Additionally, the thought of winning in front of their home crowd adds to the motivation.

“I think it’s really special to be able to host the AHA tournament, regionals and Frozen Four. We love going in front of the Roar Zone and in front of Hockey Valley,” Butze said. “Having that support and just knowing we’re going to go out and we’re going to do everything we can for our fans and for each other has just made it a lot easier.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: How will Penn State wrestling fair at the National Championships? | The Mat Return
author: The Daily Collegian wrestling staff
description:
The Daily Collegian’s Evan Smith and AJ Piazza recap the Big Ten wrestling championships and previews each weight class at this weekend’s NCAA Championships in Cleveland.

First, recapping Penn State’s success at the conference tournament at the Bryce Jordan Center. Who took home a Big Ten title? How did wrestlers who didn’t win a title perform? Which wrestlers are peaking at the right time?

Then, they dive into the upcoming National Championship brackets for each weight class. Which Nittany Lions have the best chance at securing a National Championship? How do the early round matches shake out for each wrestler? How many champions will Penn State roster in 2026?

MORE COVERAGE

title: 3 players who could benefit from big Penn State Pro Day performances
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
Pre-draft showcases continue on Wednesday morning with Penn State Pro Day.

While 10 players had the chance to make the trip to the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine, the Nittany Lions’ deep senior class features a bulk of other players looking to perform in front of scouts.

Whether they were in Indianapolis for the combine or not, some players could see their stock benefit from a big day on the turf in Holuba Hall.

Drew Allar, quarterback

Drew Allar threw in the combine and drew positive reviews, but if he chooses to compete on Wednesday, he has the opportunity to further enhance his draft stock.

Allar entered the season as a projected first-round pick, but with his season-ending ankle injury against Northwestern paired with the spiraling Penn State season, he’s now consistently mocked as a late Day 2-early Day 3 selection.

The ranking, especially after his day in Indianapolis, means there’s room to improve.

At 6-foot-5, 228 pounds, Allar has the size and arm talent to be successful. Following up on his combine performance with a big day would give way for teams earlier in Day 2 to take a chance on him, falling in love with the idea of what he could become in the league.

Devonte Ross, wide receiver

Devonte Ross wasn’t invited to Indianapolis, but he stood out as one of the Nittany Lions’ best offensive weapons last season, scoring five touchdowns and totaling 501 yards on 36 receptions.

Ross isn’t ranked in Bleacher Report’s draft big board, and Pro Football Focus slots him in as the No. 367-best prospect overall, but his athleticism could give him a boost that makes him a priority undrafted free agent or late-round draft selection.

Last year, it was cornerback Jalen Kimber who raised eyebrows at Pro Day with a 4.39-second 40-yard dash. Ross could easily surpass that time, as evidenced by his game speed on kick returns and long touchdowns both at Penn State and his former school, Troy.

Even if it isn’t for his abilities at wide receiver, Ross could easily move up a special teams coordinator’s draft board if he tests well on Wednesday.

Gabe Nwosu, punter

At 6-foot-6, 296, Gabe Nwosu will be impossible to miss when watching Penn State Pro Day — that means it’s his chance to show out.

Nwosu earned his first season as a full-time starter in 2025, beating out Riley Thompson in the offseason. It was a short sample size for the fifth-year punter, but he impressed with a 46-yard punt average, maxing out with a 68-yard boot against Michigan State.

With powerful and accurate kicks on Wednesday, Nwosu could certainly turn heads and leave room for an NFL career. Like Ross, he’s not expected to be selected in the NFL draft, but his size and skillset give him a shot late in the draft, seeking a chance to prove his worth in training camp.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Luke Lilledahl feeling confident entering his second NCAA Championships
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
At last season’s NCAA Championships, Luke Lilledahl was upset in the quarterfinals by Lehigh’s Sheldon Seymour, ending his chance at a freshman national title.

Since that bout, Lilledahl’s only loss came in an unofficial bout against teammate Nate Desmond at the Black Knight Invitational in November. He is the No. 1 seed in the 125-pound weight class for the second straight year entering the national championship, and he’s looking to right the wrongs of his loss last season.

Seymour is also undefeated, entering as the No. 4 seed on Lilledahl’s side of the bracket. In a chalk bracket, the semifinal would see a rematch with him, one of which Lilledahl is interested in competing in. When Lehigh traveled to Rec Hall this year, Seymour was out due to injury.

“I definitely wanted to get that rematch at the dual, and didn’t get it,” Lilledahl said. “So I'd be more than happy to wrestle him, and more than excited to wrestle him.”

However, the sophomore isn’t focused on his one loss — he’s focused on the ultimate goal in taking home his first national championship.

Lilledahl is entering the tournament well spoken with confidence. A season recognized as undefeated has him at the top of his game physically and mentally. In Lilledahl’s mind, he’s the top dog at 125 pounds.

“At the end of the day, my goal is to be a national champion,” Lilledahl said. “Nobody in that bracket is on my level, and if I go out there and wrestle the way I want to, that’s going to be proven.”

Since Lilledahl entered high school, he’s been prepared for high pressure events like the NCAA tournament. He wrestled at Wyoming Seminary High School, just outside of in-state Wilkes-Barre, which is one of the top wrestling schools in the country who wrestle the high school equivalent of a Big Ten schedule.

As a boarding school student, he also got a jump on the brotherhood and living aspect of college, allowing him to be fully prepared to be a one seed in his freshman year.

“I think that (Wyoming Seminary) helped me with wrestling and my personal life,” Lilledahl said. “There's really nothing that I would go back and change.”

While “Lightning Luke” will be moving at full pace in Rocket Arena, when he’s outside of the venue, Lilledahl’s goal is to distract himself from wrestling and relax. Lilledahl is hotel roommates with Shayne Van Ness on the trip, and he stated that the pair will likely play the video game Minecraft when they aren’t preparing for competition over the weekend.

Lilledahl’s perspective on wrestling is that life takes priority over a sport, and a negative result isn’t the end of the world. This mindset allows him to wrestle without unneeded pressure on his back. Rather than worry about the results, Lilledahl wants to enjoy the opportunity in front of him, while knowing the results won’t have a huge impact on his life.

“We’re going to go out there and enjoy everything because our significance in life is not determined by our wins and our losses, right?” Lilledahl said. “Whether we win or lose, our coaches are still going to love us, our families are still going to love us, and that gives us peace.”

Even if the results won’t stress Lilledahl, he has high hopes for himself and his entire team in Cleveland. Lilledahl wants the Nittany Lions to do the unthinkable and create a record that is currently unbreakable.

“Obviously, we want to have 10 national champions,” Lilledahl said.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Column | Rocco Welsh winning Big Ten title rewarded him easier path for NCAA Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
One of the biggest questions entering the NCAA Championships seeding process was how 184 pounds would look.

It was relatively unanimous that Rocco Welsh was the top seed after winning the Big Ten Championships over Minnesota’s Max McEnelly, maintaining his undefeated record.

But after the seeding controversies for the conference, nothing was fully guaranteed. Welsh had a very tough bracket during the tournament, including three wrestlers in the top 10 of the overall rankings. Now, it’s only right to factor in the fact that at 184, the Big Ten is insanely deep, so that shouldn’t have come at too much of a surprise.

But for the NCAA Championships, Welsh’s draw is rather favorable and that is for basically one reason. Welsh was heavily rewarded for his gauntlet of a Big Ten bracket.

Welsh had to face Iowa’s Angelo Ferrari in his opening match, who earned the No. 8 seed. He was dropped that low because of injuries, but a potential Big Ten and national championship matchup shouldn’t be happening in the quarterfinals.

After defeating Ferrari for the second time in tiebreakers, Welsh had to face Michigan’s Brock Mantanona in the semifinals, another wrestler who’s been inside the top 10 in the InterMat rankings all season.

Welsh took on McEnelly in the finals and went to tiebreakers with him, defeating him to remain unbeaten and earn his first Big Ten title.

The Ohio State transfer won’t have to go through nearly the run he had to in the conference tournament, as the top seed won’t likely face a big threat until the quarterfinals, and even then it’s not to the level of Ferrari, who he faced in the Big Ten quarterfinals.

If the bracket goes chalk, it’ll be Welsh against Nebraska’s Silas Allred, a match in which the Nittany Lion earned a major-decision victory in earlier this season. Based on the talents, Welsh would never major decision Ferrari, his Big Ten opponent, because of the defensive wrestling of the Hawkeye.

The most notable part of the bracket is the fact Welsh wouldn’t have to wrestle any of Ferrari, McEnelly or Missouri’s Aeoden Sinclair until the finals if he makes it there. In the semifinals, his biggest threat is Mantanona or Franklin and Marshall’s James Conway. Welsh wrestled Conway in 2025, where he came out on top, 4-2, but that was during Welsh’s redshirt year at Ohio State.

The Big Ten is the most dominant conference in the country, and Welsh winning one of the deepest classes of the 10 weights deservedly rewards him for still being undefeated.

With the wrestlers he faced in the Big Ten Championships, Welsh’s early path is significantly easier, and very favorable for a chance at his first national title.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Staff predictions | How Penn State wrestling will perform at the NCAA Championships
author: Wrestling staff
description:
Time to bring home some hardware.

Penn State will travel to Cleveland for the NCAA Championships at Rocket Arena this week, with the action kicking off on Thursday.

Here’s how our staff thinks the Nittany Lion wrestlers will fare.

Lyle Alenstein: 5 individual champions/team title

Once again, the team title will be a lock for Penn State, as the Nittany Lions will win their fifth national championship in a row, doing so for the first time in program history after winning four consecutive titles on three separate occasions.

As far as the winners for Penn State, it’ll be Marcus Blaze, Shayne Van Ness, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines and Josh Barr.

Van Ness, Mesenbrink, Haines and Barr, in my eyes, should have an easy time en route to standing at the top of the podium, cruising to a national title victory.

My final pick to win it all will be Blaze, as he’ll recover from losing in the Big Ten Championships final and win it all on the national stage. He suffered a defeat to Ohio State’s Ben Davino, who he also beat in the regular season, and very well could see him in the national bracket.

There’s the idea that Blaze lost as part of the “Cael tax” in reference to Cael Sanderson, but I believe he’ll get over that hump with a win at the national level in his freshman year.

Luke Lilledahl and Rocco Welsh would be next picks to win, but will come up short in talented fields.

Andrew Deal: 6 individual champions/team title

The team title looks to be a lock for the Nittany Lions, as seven of the wrestlers hold No. 1 spots. Penn State has always performed well, and it’s pretty safe to say this team could be the best team yet.

For the winners, PJ Duke, Lilledahl, Van Ness, Mesenbrink, Haines and Barr all hold strong chances, and from my perspective, will stand atop the podium Saturday night.

Mesenbrink and Barr seem to be borderline locks, as both have bonused their way through the entire season. This includes bonus-point victories against highly ranked wrestlers in Iowa’s Mikey Caliendo and Little Rock’s Stephen Little.

Lilledahl seems to be on another level this season and has the confidence, and now the experience to win the title, while Van Ness has his best opportunity to win it all, although the only thing in his way is himself. On multiple occasions, he’s gone down at least by one takedown, but has showcased enough gas to be able to get back in these matches.

For Duke and Haines, they’ve dealt with tough matchups against Nebraska wrestlers, but have come out victorious against each of them. It could mean some difficulties in the tournament, but the persistence of the two will prevail again.

AJ Piazza: 7 Individual champions/team title

It looks like Penn State will make it five years in a row of winning the NCAA team title. The team has never won five straight, but it has won four in a row three different times. Penn State has seven No. 1 seeds as the championships begin, and I have the Nittany Lions coming home with seven individual champions.

A few of these winners are locks, which includes Mesenbrink, Haines, and Barr. All three of these wrestlers have dominated their opponents and have shown that they’re on a different level compared to the rest of their weight classes.

Lilledahl and Van Ness have been on top of their weight classes for the entire season, but there is some competition that made me hesitant. Lilledahl and Sheldon Seymour from Lehigh could potentially match up again, but Lilledahl seems ready for it. Van Ness doesn’t have anyone else in the way, but he’s gone down early in a few matches recently, but continues to comeback and show why he’s the No. 1 wrestler at 149.

Duke seems to have peaked at the right time. The freshman dominated both Ohio State’s Brandon Cannon and Nebraska’s Antrell Taylor at the Big Ten Championships — that was all the convincing I needed to believe the freshman would become a national champion in Year 1.

The toughest pick for me is Blaze. The top-three wrestlers at 133 include Blaze, Davino, and Oklahoma State’s Jax Forrest. Blaze and Forrest could be the semifinal match, and if Blaze gets by Forrest, I believe he will have his revenge match against Davino and take home the national championship at 133.

Evan Smith: 6 individual champions/team title

I’m going to say a shocking sentence. Penn State will win the team national championship. Surprising, I know, but I think this is the year.

I’ve been going back and forth on so many of these weight classes for the past week, but this feels like a season Penn State can break the five national championship marker.

For me, I have one of Blaze or Duke securing a national title in their freshman year. It’s essentially a toss-up, but I’m leaning towards Duke due to the stacked nature of the 133-pound weight class. Duke avenged his only loss to Taylor earlier in the season, and has looked miles ahead of his opponents.

Lilledahl is entering this tournament as confident as he’s ever been, giving me confidence to pick him in an interesting weight class. Seymour, who he lost to last year, is also undefeated while defending national champion Vincent Robinson from NC State is all the way down at the 12th seed.

Van Ness and Barr simply have no contenders around them that scare me entering this weekend. Barr, I expect to waltz his way to a national title, while Van Ness I’m slightly more tepid on after his run at last year’s tournament.

At 165 pounds, Mesenbrink wins easily. Haines is in the same boat in his final weekend as a Nittany Lion. At 184 pounds, I predict Welsh to win what is arguably the most loaded weight class. Although his run to the Big Ten title was uninspiring, his reaction to it lends me belief he will peak in Cleveland in a field of competitors that he’s faced most of the best of.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball goes winless during week in West Coast
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a West Coast road trip filled with ranked opponents, Penn State’s three-match road series ended with a winless 0-3 outcome.

The blue and white opened the week with a 3-0 loss against No. 2 Long Beach State. The second game saw a four-set loss to No. 6 Pepperdine, and the week concluded with a sweep against No. 4 USC.

After falling short against the three teams, the Nittany Lions moved to 11-7 on the year.

Here’s a look at what happened during their road trip.

Serving issues

The blue and white left the West Coast with a mere nine service aces across the three games played. The matches against Long Beach State and USC saw one ace each, while the Pepperdine match saw seven.

However, the Nittany Lions tallied a total of 41 service errors across the three games. Gaige Gabriel had two aces against Pepperdine, his only two of the week, but logged a total of nine service errors.

In general, serving has remained a weaker part of the blue and white’s game this season. It currently totals 83 service aces, while also totaling 274 service errors.

This week was no different.

Allowing too many aces for the opposition has also been an issue for the blue and white. Across the three West Coast games, Penn State allowed 29 service aces.

The blue and white’s leader in aces this year, Owen Rose, only put up one ace in all three games. Despite leading the team, Rose ranks No. 61 in the NCAA in aces per set. Notably, the redshirt senior is the highest-ranked Nittany Lion in this category.

Ranked No. 27 in the NCAA in aces per set, there’s definitely room for improvement for the Penn State squad.

That’s not to say it’s all negative. Tristan Hassell tallied three service aces against Pepperdine. With 10 aces on the year, and only a freshman, his future with the Nittany Lions looks bright.

Blocking struggles

The Nittany Lions entered the week of West Coast games as the No. 3 team in the NCAA in blocks per set. In fact, the number of blocks they have put up this season has been remarkable.

Most notably, Rose broke a school record earlier this season after tallying 14 blocks in a match against Harvard.

The middle blocker currently totals 81 blocks on the year and entered the road trip at No. 2 in the NCAA in blocks per set, eventually claiming the top spot in the nation.

However, Rose didn’t seem to make a whole lot of difference over the three games when playing some of the best programs in the country. It’s essential that some of the blue and white’s best players make the difference going forward.

Rose only had six total blocks against Long Beach State, three against Pepperdine and two against USC.

The responsibility of making necessary blocks doesn’t just fall upon the shoulders of Rose, however.

Gabriel, the second leader in blocks for Penn State, put up one block against Long Beach State and two against Pepperdine and USC.

Even if the star middle blockers are ignored, the Nittany Lions as a team only produced 11 total blocks in their first match against Long Beach State and had the same number against USC.

With only five total blocks against Pepperdine, with none in the first set of the match, the blue and white didn’t provide the necessary defense that was needed against some of the top schools in the country.

The blue and white won’t be back in action until 6 p.m. on March 25, where it will look to get back on a good note against Ohio State in Pittsburgh.

title: History of Penn State wrestling in NCAA Championships
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
The NCAA Championships are coming up and Penn State has another year of high expectations. The Nittany Lions have seven No. 1 seeds and are looking to win their fifth straight national championship.

Here’s a look at some history of Penn State’s previous performances as the NCAA Championship is approaching.

NCAA Championships

Penn State has won four straight national championships on three separate occasions. The Nittany Lions have won from 2022-25, 2016-19, and 2011-14. This season the squad will look to win it all five times in a row.

The most points Penn State ever put up was with last season’s team, and that is the most all time with 177 points. Last season, Penn State had 10 All-Americans, which was the second team to ever do so. Minnesota did it first in 2001.

The biggest point differential was with the 2024 team. Penn State put up 172.5 points meanwhile the next highest team was Cornell, who had 72.5 points in the tournament. The Nittany Lions took home the team title by 100 points, which is the largest-point gap in NCAA history.

Single-season national champions

This season, Penn State will look to break the record that they are tied with Iowa and Oklahoma State for, and that’s five national champions in one season.

The Nittany Lions have had this milestone happen twice. The first time was in 2017, with Zain Reatherford capping off an undefeated season with a tech fall victory in the national championship at 149 pounds. At 157, Jason Nolf also ended undefeated, and won by major decision in the finals.

At 165, Vincenzo Joseph took home the national championship with a pin. At 174, Mark Hall had a big upset as the No. 5 and won the national championship. At 184, it was a No. 1 vs No. 2 matchup in Gabe Dean of Cornell vs Bo Nickal. It was a tight match, but Nickal got the win by decision.

The other time the Nittany Lions had five national champions was in 2022. Roman Bravo-Young went undefeated and beat Daton Fix of Oklahoma State in the finals. Bravo-Young wasn’t the only wrestler to go undefeated, as now assistant coach Nick Lee also went undefeated and defeated No. 15 Kizhan Clarke of North Carolina in the finals, 10-3.

Carter Starocci took home the title at 174 pounds in tiebreakers against Mekhi Lewis of Virginia Tech. At 184, Aaron Brooks made his way to the championship and won by decision, 5-3. To make it three in a row for the Nittany Lions, Max Dean took home the championship at 197 over Jacob Warner of Iowa in a close match, 3-2.

Carter Starocci five-time champ

Penn State had the first ever five-time national champion end his collegiate career last season, and that’s Carter Starocci. Starocci won national championships at both 174 and 184 pounds during his time in State College. He took home four national championships at 174 pounds and his final national championship was at 184.

The Erie, Pennsylvania, native held a record of 119-4 during his time with the Nittany Lions. For his first national title, Starocci avenged a loss against Michael Kemerer of Iowa, who defeated him in the Big Ten Championship.

His first undefeated season was in 2021-22, and he capped it off with a victory in tiebreakers against Mekhi Lewis of Virginia Tech. Starocci continued his reign in dominant fashion. In 2022-23 he defeated Mikey Labriola of Nebraska with a pin early in the match.

In 2023-24, Starocci faced current Nittany Lion Rocco Welsh in the final. The Ohio State transfer couldn’t get anything going on the attack, as Starocci controlled the match and got the win 2-0.

To end his collegiate career, Starocci bumped up a weight class and took on the national champion in Parker Keckeisen of Northern Iowa. It was one of the closer matches Starocci ever wrestled in, but he came away with the victory by decision, 4-3, and became the first ever five-time national champion.

title: Key position battles on Penn State’s defense headed into spring ball
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Spring ball is starting soon for Penn State, so a first look at position battles for the 2026 season is coming up.

On the defensive side of the ball, a mix of returnees and transfer portal additions leaves some spots on the depth chart up in the air. Special teams, meanwhile, are more set in stone with expected starters.

Here’s a look at those potential position battles.

Defensive end

Max Granville had buzz before the 2025 season, but a long-term injury endured last summer caused him to miss the year. Now coming back to Penn State for his redshirt sophomore campaign, Granville is an expected starter at defensive end.

The starting spot opposite of Granville should have some competition, though. Yvan Kemajou is back for his sophomore year after making some flashes in 2025, tallying 13 tackles, five tackles for loss and 1.5 sacks.

Colorado transfer Alexander McPherson could also make a push to earn a starting role as he showed promise in his freshman year last season with 16 tackles, six quarterback hurries, one blocked punt and half a sack. Iowa State transfer Ikenna Ezeogu has a veteran presence in the position group as a redshirt senior, and he posted 25 tackles, three tackles for loss, a sack and a fumble recovery in 2025.

Defensive tackle

The interior of Penn State’s defensive line will look different the upcoming season as 2025 starters Zane Durant and Alonzo Ford Jr. both departed. Redshirt seniors Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams could be in line to start with their experience.

Taupaki and Williams both transferred in from UCLA earlier this offseason. Williams had 39 tackles while Taupaki went for 15 tackles and a sack last year.

There are also transfers Dallas Vakalahi and Armstrong Nnodim, who could make some noise for the starting jobs. Vakalahi had 27 tackles and two sacks in two seasons with Utah, while Nnodim tallied 11 tackles in 2025.

Competing with them is redshirt junior Ty Blanding, who’s said he’s looking to take another step in his development this offseason after seeing action in 13 games and recording nine tackles last season.

Safety

Iowa State transfer Marcus Neal Jr. seems to have a starting role at safety solidified, but there could be a battle for the other starting role between Jeremiah Cooper and Jamison Patton.

Cooper moved to cornerback in 2025, but his season was cut short due to a knee injury, totaling nine tackles and an interception in three games prior. The redshirt senior has 134 tackles and eight interceptions in his career and returns to safety this season.

Patton, who’s entering his senior year, started nine games in 2025. He’s accumulated 79 tackles and four interceptions in his career.

title: Column | Hooking up isn’t harmless
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
College students often lament the infamous “hook-up culture” permeating our campuses. And for good reason.

The most intimate of experiences has also become the most casual. If it sounds oxymoronic, that’s because it is. The mixing of alcohol and lowered inhibitions with something so deeply personal is, unsurprisingly, often a poor mix.

But in the same breath, many of these students extol the supposed virtues that said culture provides. You’ll often hear claims of female freedom, character growth, or even a simple test drive before you purchase the real thing.

And while we’re on the topic of metaphors, we should probably stop being surprised when the benefactors of hook-up culture don’t buy the cow.

In my own experiences with friends, I hear the same lines about female empowerment and hook-up culture in public, and then hear the sadness and disappointment these hook-ups have caused in private.

What’s most telling about this phenomenon isn’t necessarily who participates in it, as it’s pushed everywhere from TV shows to campus events, but the dueling narratives it creates in front of and behind closed doors.

Why, when young people are more starved for genuine connection than ever before, do we continue to play into the most superficial of all connections? Why do we trade a singular moment of possible pleasure for the much longer-lasting pain of regret?

I often hear the (also oxymoronic) phrase “harmless hook-up.” There’s simply no such thing.

I could bore you with ample statistics to back this up, but the most incriminating evidence of all is that those who claim harmlessness continue to speak out of both sides of their mouths. This doublespeak is used more often than not.

For example, Susan Kraus, a therapist, wrote that collegiate women involved in hook-up culture share that they both love the feeling of being picked by a man, and also feel utterly objectified in a column published in the Medium. She also said that men enjoy this culture simply because they can, but then also end up dissatisfied.

An article in The Atlantic titled “ Boys on the Side” published in 2012 calls hook-up culture “an engine of female progress — one being harnessed and driven by women themselves,” while also sharing stories of women being actively degraded by others sharing photos of their sexual activity and lamenting hearing men share their sexual escapades pursuant to this culture’s norms.

Even liberal collegiate women’s publications like Her Campus aren’t immune to this paradox. Authors both praise the liberality of being able to be with anyone at any time and then bemoan the obvious consequences of such actions, like STDs and decreased self-worth.

In an age of supposedly radical ideas that are really just the same old sexual liberation talking points, try something truly radical: save yourself for someone who’s worth it when the time is right.

The positives are endless. And so are the negatives of hooking up.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Column | Why people love raunchy television
author: Emma Strickling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Just last month, Netflix released the second half of Bridgerton’s latest season. Within this book-to-screen adaptation, there’s plenty of drama and, of course, a lavish ball or two. However, probably the most commonly associated aspect of the show is its raunchy scenes.

According to Variety, season four amassed 39.7 million views within its first four days of being released. Bridgerton is one of Netflix's most popular shows, with seasons one and three ranked seventh and ninth, respectively, on their most-watched list of all time.

Similar patterns have been seen with other shows that are quite steamy, such as “Heated Rivalry,” which chronicles the love story of two closeted male hockey players. By the time the final episode of the show was released, the amount of streaming minutes grew to 324 million streaming minutes in comparison to its debut week figure of 30 million minutes.

Raunchy television seems to be incredibly popular, but why?

It’s clear that sex sells, whether you think that’s immoral or not. Shows on the raunchier side get people talking, either through conversations with friends or in social media comment sections.

While perhaps viewers come by these shows by chance, they could also be seeking them out to fill a void — especially Gen Z viewers. Recent data trends show that Gen Z is engaging less in sexual activity and is also dating less. Roughly 24% of adults 18 to 29 had abstained from sex within the past year, according to USA Today, a figure that’s doubled since 2010. While adults under 30 had the highest percentage of a single population at 47%, according to Forbes.

Perhaps these types of shows act as a way to still engage with sex without having to actually engage with it in their personal lives. The dating world poses many risks, which the supposedly risk-averse Gen Z are trying to avoid. Therefore, these shows offer a sense of control.

It’s acting as an alternative, especially since these shows often depict intimacy more naturally and authentically.

While I can’t speak for the shows I haven’t seen, from the shows I am familiar with, like “Bridgerton” and “Heated Rivalry,” there’s more than hot, steamy sex scenes.Once the passion has cooled down, what’s left is a relationship between two people. A relationship of yearning and emotional connection. Which is, unfortunately, something not present in every relationship.

These shows are an escape from a relationship that’s not hitting the bar and into a relationship that the viewer might desire in some aspect. In that long duration, it’s almost as if the viewer is able to be present in the world in front of them and forget about the problems of their own love life.

Once the show is over, it’s back to reality. Which might be why whenever the latest raunchy show comes out, it continues to have a fairly large following until after it ends.

Although it’s a place for an escape, these TV shows ultimately can’t develop the social skills or the same feelings that real-life relationships do. While it might fill a void temporarily, it’s not a successful long-term solution.

If there’s an actual desire to date, but fear is overshadowing it, take a deep breath. Consider what is causing that fear. Is it worth not taking your shot or even simply just trying?

Unlike these TV shows that will be available in some form or another, the opportunities to make something happen romantically aren’t always going to be. Sometimes you just have to take a leap of faith. But even if there’s rejection, at least you’ll know instead of always wondering “what if.”

MORE COLUMNS

title: State College Borough, Penn State collaborate on local sustainability efforts
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Through borough policies and partnerships with Penn State and other organizations, State College has taken several steps to advance sustainability initiatives over the years.

Peter Buck, the director for education for Penn State Sustainability (PSS), said the borough has already passed several resolutions to aid local sustainability efforts, such as Resolution 944, which declared the borough a climate protection community.

Buck said State College and the Sustainable Communities Collaborative (SCC), an outreach and scholarship program within PSS, have worked together on several projects for over a decade from promoting bicycle and pedestrian safety to solar assessments.

Many of the projects are displayed during the Campus and Community Sustainability Expo, held at the end of each semester. This year’s expo is on April 30 in the State College Municipal Building. Buck said many of the projects have had a sizable impact on State College residents even if they weren’t aware of them.

“The corner of Park Avenue and Atherton Street is notoriously dangerous and part of it has to do with pedestrian and not car traffic and so (the SCC) organized a group of classes, three in a row, that worked on messaging to make sure that pedestrians and bicyclists are safe and you might think, ‘Well, why is that sustainability?’” Buck said. “Well, health and well-being are part of sustainability to transportation-related issues.”

He said collaborations such as these help maintain good relations between the borough and the university.

“It is great to have this way of bringing a few hundred students a year, actually every semester, in touch with the borough in an incredibly positive, socially, educationally and economically productive way,” Buck said.

Buck said the borough also participates in larger sustainability and climate actions in collaboration with organizations like the Centre Region Council of Governments (CRCOG).

Most notably, State College has worked with the CRCOG on projects like the Centre Region Climate Action and Adaptation Plan, meant to make the borough and other involved municipalities more sustainable through various means such as land management and waste reduction.

The borough also planned on implementing infrastructure as part of the Solar Power Purchase Agreement (SPPA). The SPPA aimed to increase the presence of solar power in Centre County as a way to maintain low energy costs and expand renewable energy sources both in and around the borough.

However, Prospect 14, the company that developed the SPPA, raised concerns of economic inviability due to the repealing of economic incentives related to solar power by the federal government, alongside tariffs on solar panels. As a result, the borough voted unanimously to terminate its contract with the company set to provide electricity from the SPPA during a council meeting on March 5

Buck — who contributed to the SPPA as a member of its working group — said he believed the efforts by the federal government in curbing sustainability measures will only inspire local government officials to do more to accomplish sustainability goals.

“If anything, there is more willpower in the State College area because of the challenges brought from Washington,” Buck said. “People will find a way forward on sustainability and climate action.”

Buck said the SPPA is the only sustainability program affected in the region by federal administrative changes or policies.

According to Ilona Ballreich, SCC’s program director, its relationship with State College is mutually beneficial, as the borough gets the resources needed to maintain sustainability and students are given opportunities to get in-the-field experience and gain community connections.

“Let's say community partners are interested in solar but they don't really have the staff in-house, they don't have a solar engineer or a solar expert,” Ballreich said. “I can then work with a group of students in a solar design class, who have the technical knowledge.”

Ballreich said it’s important for the university and the borough to continue to work together on the issue of sustainability in order to best serve both residents and students.

“At the end of the day, I hope that we all want to make the world a better place for everybody, so that's kind of my mantra,” Ballreich said. “That's what SCC likes to do and tries to do … make the world the best it can possibly be for all involved.”

State College Borough Council Member Gopal Balachandran, who also represents the borough on the CRCOG’s climate action and sustainability committee, said the dialogue between the borough and Penn State is helpful for planning sustainability initiatives.

“We end up talking about various efforts to promote awareness to also be able to gain greater climate and resilience,” Balachandran said. “A lot of it is just policy-based work as well as educational work involving things like solar power, so we work with the university on a lot of those things.”

Balachandran said one of the borough’s current projects is attempting to conduct a greenhouse gas inventory to measure emissions produced in State College.

He said he believes the borough is at an advantage due to Penn State’s willingness to assist with sustainability efforts.

“They have so many experts at the university, and so we're able to draw upon that expertise, especially when we have limited staff resources,” Balachandran said. “So many of us are involved in the university in one form or another, and I think we're very lucky to have that.”

MORE BOROUGH COVERAGE

title: Review | Bruno Mars returns as ‘The Romantic’
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
Bruno Mars recently released his fifth studio album, “The Romantic,” which debuted at No. 1 on Billboard's Top 200 after a five-year break since his last project.

The 40-year-old pop artist has a groundbreaking musical career spanning over a decade and a half, with each album gaining considerable notoriety at its release. His latest album takes a mature and mellowed out turn different from his traditional pop and hip-hop heavy discography.

“The Romantic” is a purely feel-good album. Featuring nine tracks with a run time of just over a half hour, every song on this album is made to make your heart skip a beat and reminisce about the cherished memories of lovers and passionate romance.

There are songs that will make you dance, songs that will make you swoon and songs that will make you nostalgic. As the album suggests, each song has romantic connotations and fills every second with intimate bliss — even the break-up ballads.

The album as a whole has an incredibly experimental feel when taking his previous albums into account. There are Latin inspirations that give the project a sincerely soulful and somewhat sedated feel, while still inducing perfect levels of punch behind every verse. Combining these characteristics, Mars leaves us with a modern take on popular 80’s R&B that give hints of prime Marvin Gaye, Prince and Michael Jackson, both musically and lyrically.

Mars, a veteran of crafting an ambitious and win-at-all-costs love song, opens up the album with the pleading “Risk It All.” On this track, Mars unloads his romantic prowess and commitment to doing whatever is asked of him to hold the hand he seeks. “I would swim across the sea just to show you / Sacrifice my life just to hold you / I could go on and on to prove that you belong / Here in my arms.”

On the proceeding tracks, “Cha-Cha-Cha,” “I Just Might,” and “God Was Showing Off,” Mars leads you through a series of love-confessionals that feel like a collection of everything he’s wanted to write since the start of his career.

Lyrically, these songs hold the same weight and charm as previous hits like “Grenade” and “Locked Out Of Heaven,” but presenting them in a more professional manner through the pristine production of his band.

What Mars did with this album is structure it as if it was the soundtrack to an actual relationship. The first half of the album opens with so much sheer joy and passion for love in its simplest form — as if those beginning tracks were just the essence of what true love really feels like.

Those feelings are hard to put into words but Mars finds a way to do it and back them up with a fantastic ensemble. The back half of the album speaks to the end of a relationship, with fights, tension and resentment all under the shade of a desperate call for resolution.

Songs like “Why You Wanna Fight” and “Something Serious” are songs that cry out to the fleeting feeling of love and all of its much welcomed intricacies and perks. They’re songs that plead for immortal adoration while reconciling with the notion that it all might, in fact, be gone despite the effort.

There's a much stronger presence of the band and a sense of communal effort on this album, compared to his previous albums that really kept Mars in the focus through his radio-friendly style of production. With “The Romantic,” Mars creates a project with a lot more camaraderie and soul that permeates through every song.

The whole album is relatively low-key, oozing “late-night-smooching-by-the-fireplace-vibes.” Mars’ use of bongos, trumpets, jazzy drums and a harmonious set of backup singers, gives every song the feel of a dramatic motion picture, moving from one element to the next and then blending them all together with a seamless and groovy end product that feels like you were just whisked away by your loved one.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Breaking down the online phenomenon for Penn State wrestling known as ‘Cael tax’
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
It’s tax season.

For two Penn State wrestlers, their “taxes” have already been paid this season, but it wasn’t the typical taxes. PJ Duke and Marcus Blaze have both paid their dues for the “Cael tax” leading up to the NCAA Championships.

The term “Cael tax” has become popular on the internet, alluding to the idea that each of Cael Sanderson’s wrestlers have to metaphorically pay a tax — a loss.

This comes because of Sanderson’s historic collegiate wrestling career, where he didn’t lose a single match in his four years as a starter at Iowa State. Now, Sanderson leads one of the most historic dynasties in all of sports, which is entering uncharted territory, but one thing that hasn’t come yet is an undefeated, four-time champion.

Each of the wrestlers under Sanderson at Penn State have lost at least one match, with a few only having that single number in the loss column. But, it’s continued to remain true through this season with each of the wrestlers having to “pay their tax.”

Blaze and Duke were some of the highest regarded high school prospects in recent years, being ranked the No. 1 and No. 2 wrestlers, respectively in the 2025 class, per FloWrestling, and were poised to escape the Cael tax in their first season.

However, Duke fell to Nebraska’s Antrell Taylor during the dual season, before quickly getting his redemption in the Big Ten finals, dominating Taylor to take the 157-pound belt.

Blaze avoided his first threat of the tax, when he escaped with a tiebreaker victory over Ohio State’s Ben Davino. However, Davino handed Blaze his tax in the Big Ten finals at 133 pounds, when he defeated the Nittany Lion in sudden victory. There’s a chance at immediate redemption in the NCAA Championships if both Blaze and Davino make it to the semifinals.

Sanderson has coached two four-time champions in Carter Starocci and Aaron Brooks, with Starocci winning an illustrious fifth title. But neither wrestler went undefeated. Starocci finished with a 104-4 record, while Brooks ended his career with an 89-3 record, both some of the smallest number of loss totals in a blue and white singlet.

All of the starters have paid the Cael tax, with the most “expensive” being Mitchell Mesenbrink’s loss. Mesenbrink fell in the finals of the NCAA Championships against Iowa State’s David Carr in 2024. The only loss of Mesenbrink’s redshirt freshman season is still the only loss of his career to this date.

Levi Haines has been a unique wrestler under Sanderson. While Haines has not redshirted at all, and has wrestled four straight years in the starting lineup, he still couldn’t avoid paying his tax, netting an early loss in his career.

In his second match at Penn State, Haines fell to Northern Colorado’s Vincent Zerban, 8-6, in the Bearcat Open at Binghamton. The Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native has gone 94-4 thus far and has a chance to get to 99 wins in his career if he wins his second title this weekend.

No matter the talent when entering the Penn State wrestling room. No matter the amount of development each wrestler garners. No matter the historic accolades earned, no wrestler has evaded the infamous tax courtesy of Sanderson.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey discusses Frozen Four, team culture
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey’s 2025-26 season continues to make history. After hosting an outdoor game at Beaver Stadium, winning a fourth-consecutive AHA Championship and hosting its first-ever NCAA Regional, the Nittany Lions defeated No. 6 UConn, 3-0, to advance to a program-first Frozen Four appearance.

The blue and white — the hosts of the 2026 Women’s Frozen Four — are set to face No. 2 Wisconsin at Pegula Ice Arena on Friday at 7:30 p.m.

Ahead of the biggest game in Penn State women’s hockey history thus far, coach Jeff Kampersal, forward Maddy Christian and defenseman Kendall Butze took to the podium to discuss the implications of the upcoming weekend.

Frozen Four

Penn State is the lone program to make its debut in this year’s Frozen Four, with Wisconsin, Ohio State and Northeastern all having played in multiple in the past. The Badgers and the Buckeyes have gone all the way to the national championship game together for the past three years, with Wisconsin winning in 2023 and 2025 while Ohio State won in 2024.

This is the second time Pegula Ice Arena has hosted the Frozen Four, having previously hosted it in 2022.

“(Hosting has) been in the back of our heads since freshman year, to be completely honest,” Butze said. “We've known we were going to host the 2026 Frozen Four for quite some time now. Trying to get the program to a position where we can actually make that a realistic goal for ourselves was something that we've been talking about for a few years now."

With a graduating class of six seniors and two graduate students, the Nittany Lions’ postseason success began with the freshman season for the Class of 2026. The senior class propelled Penn State into the limelight with conference success and four consecutive national tournament appearances.

Now — with Penn State’s core being mature — Kampersal’s squad found national tournament success as well, winning its first national tournament game in program history.

“Coming in, freshman year, freshman summer, and we all were just best friends,” Christian said. “Instantly. We all clicked. We all connected. Everybody is a different human at different times, but we also are different hockey players and I think that's what makes us so good, because we appreciate one another so much. We've gone through this together.”

Team culture

When asked about the biggest achievements this season, Christian noted that the “culture” actively aided the on-ice success.

“The women in our locker room are really loud,” Kampersal said. “They get along with each other. And it's, it's the loudest team that I've ever coached, and that's been a long time. They’re helpful, academically, socially, whatever it is on the ice, they're there for one another. That connectivity matters when we hit the ice, too.”

Team chemistry is important. Whether it be on-ice passing or off-ice relationships, that connection is what breeds teamwork. Butze added her experience with the team’s on and off-ice relationship.

“I know that we put a lot of work in,” Butze said. We meet weekly as a team. We really enjoy each other's company. We hang out off the ice so much, which isn't different from previous years, but it's just something that's built the connection between upperclassmen, freshmen and sophomores, which I think is really important."

Community support

It’s no secret that Penn State doesn’t draw the same crowds that the other programs get. The program record for attendance was set at Beaver Stadium with 1,838 spectators. However, with the Frozen Four coming to Hockey Valley, the Pegula Ice Arena is primed to host an electric crowd for the top four teams in the country.

“We have a really dynamite team, and still struggle to fill Pegula,” Kampersal said. “People go to a women's volleyball game — deservedly so; they're amazing athletes — and they fill up 5,000 fans and then maybe (the men’s volleyball team) doesn’t get as much. So I'm not sure why there's a differential, but hopefully on Friday, Hockey Valley will show up and support because they deserve it."

The current home indoor attendance record was set on Oct. 6, 2018, with a crowd of 1,804, when Penn State faced Merrimack. Recently, the Nittany Lions drew a crowd of 1,615 for the regional final game against UConn.

“It's really special to be able to host the AHA Tournament, NCAA Regionals and the Frozen Four,” Butze said. “We love playing in front of the Roar Zone, in front of Hockey Valley. Having that support and just knowing we're gonna go out and we're gonna do everything we can for our fans and for each other has just made it a lot easier.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE
