Two other pitchers who might start games this weekend are right-handers Ben Hudson and Isaiah Shayter. Hudson provided a quality outing in Penn State’s 9-3 win over Grand Canyon, but his three starts since have been rocky.

The West Virginia transfer holds a 7.36 ERA, while surrendering 18 hits and 15 earned runs. Shayter’s 14.34 ERA was ballooned after his last start in the blue and white’s 14-4 loss against Texas Tech.

The freshman went just 1.2 innings and allowed five earned runs off five hits and five walks. So, the Elizabeth Town, Pennsylvania, native must last longer if he gets a start against Iowa.

Players to watch

The Hawkeyes’ lineup is stacked throughout, but there are some notable hitters the Nittany Lions must contain. Mitch Wood is one of the best all-around batters in the Big Ten.

The senior possesses a .414 average, 1.330 OPS, 12 hits and 15 RBI. Iowa doesn’t hit home runs often, but he leads the team in the category with three.

Gable Mitchell spearheads the offense with a team-best .484 average, 31 hits, 17 RBI and 12 walks.

While Penn State’s offense ranks in the bottom three for many key offensive stats in the Big Ten, Bryce Molinaro was a bright spot in its two-game series against Georgetown.

The redshirt junior homered in both games and brought his home run total to four. Michael Anderson continued his hot start and smashed his team-best seventh home run in Wednesday’s 5-4 win against the Hoyas.

Iowa’s pitching staff enters the series with a 5.18 team ERA, allowing 133 hits and 80 earned runs. The Nittany Lions will likely face the Hawkeyes’ ace, right-hander Maddux Frese.

The junior owns a 2.50 ERA, totaling 13 strikeouts, while also surrendering just 15 hits, 3 walks and five earned runs over 18 innings.

Penn State’s pitching and offense must click together to record its first series win this weekend.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball drops second game against Georgetown, going winless in series
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State baseball faced off against Georgetown in Washington, D.C., in its final game of a two-series matchup. Ultimately, Penn State would lose to Georgetown 5-4.

Penn State started out the day in the batter's box. Outfielder Cohl Mercado fouled out to start the order. Infielder Michael Anderson flew out, followed by infielder Spencer Barnett, who went down swinging. A strong start for Georgetown right-handed pitcher JT Rabb.

Nittany Lions freshman right-handed pitcher Ethan Bauerschmidt started the game on the mound in the bottom of the first. The Hoyas started off hot with a triple down the right field line by outfielder Ashtin Gilio.

Outfielder Travis Ilitch continued with a single. Infielder and pitcher Connor Peek flew out to center field, but that allowed Gilio to make it home for the game's first run. Bauerschmidt threw one out, and catcher Connor Price grounded out to end the inaugural inning.

Penn State fired back quickly, coming from a Bryce Molinaro home run to right-center field. Though the hot start would fizzle with two groundouts, Preston Yaucher went down swinging. Raab let up a double from Jayden Davis and walked Joey DeMucci, who went on to steal second base.

The second stint on defense for the blue and white was a fast one. Bauerschmidt struck out the first two Hoyas batters looking. Then, infielder AJ Solomon popped one up to shortstop to end the second inning. The score was tied 1-1 going into the third.

Back to the top of the order for the Nittany Lions, Mercado flew out to start the hitting. Anderson and Barnett hit back-to-back homers to center and right-center, respectively. Molinaro grounded out after his homerun in the last inning and Jack Porter struck out swinging. Penn State led 3-1, only scoring by going the distance.

Gilio flew out on his second at-bat. Ilitch singled through the right side to get back on the positive for Georgetown. Bauerschmidt hit Peek with a pitch to advance the runners. Jeremy Sheffield moved the runners again after an error from shortstop Yaucher.

Christian Hamilton reached on a fielder's choice, advancing the runners and allowing Ilitch to score unearned. Hamilton was then caught stealing to end the inning. Penn State still led 3-2 after an inning of mishaps.

The Hoyas started the 4th with a pitching change; they swapped Rabb for freshman right-handed pitcher Ashton Arroyo. Davis got to first with a bunt, followed by Jesse Jaconski getting hit by a pitch. A Yaucher single led to a Davis score and Jaconski getting thrown out when he tried to advance to third.

Georgetown made another pitching change from Arroyo to left-handed pitcher Andrew Jergins, now their third of the game. Yaucher got caught stealing second to end Penn State's at-bats.

Price was walked, then got to second with the help of Dylan Larkins bunt, a sacrifice play. Dante Pozzi hit a homer to left field for a two-run play. After that, the Hoyas had two groundouts to end the 4th. The score stood tied up 4-4 going into the 5th inning.

Mercado struck out swinging and Anderson got the first hit for the Nittany Lions in the inning. Barnett then grounded a ball into a double play. A quick inning of batting for Penn State.

A pitching change got Bauerschmidt off the mound and gave right-handed pitcher Braden Leek his chance. Coach Mike Gambino also made changes to catcher and first base; he moved Avery Smith to catcher and DeMucci to first.

Leed began his pitching with a walk for Ilitch, who got caught stealing for an out. Peek lined out, and Sheffield swung out to end a fast offensive series for Georgetown.

The Hoyas made another pitching change; they took out Jergins and tried graduate student Griffin O’Connor. Molinaro popped up for the first out of the 6th inning. Porter was walked and Davis singled. The advancements stopped after Jaconski and DeMucci both flew out.

Penn State also began its defensive 6th inning with a pitching change. Out went Leed and in went lefty Logan Olsen. Hamilton singled to first and Price struck out looking. Olsen walked Larkins and both Price and Larkins stole second and third. Olsen messed up again, hitting Dante Pozzi with a pitch.

After a rough start for Olsen, he got switched out for righty Kyle Emmons. Solomon struck out looking, followed by a groundout from Gilio to end the 6th. The score remained tied 4-4.

O’Connor continued his nice start; he made quick work of the 7th. Yaucher grounded out to second, Mercado did the same, and Anderson went down swinging,

The Nittany Lions made yet another pitching change. This time, they swapped Emmons for Robert Brown III, a freshman right-handed pitcher. Brown III started off well, Ilitch and Peek both grounded out and Sheffield singled. Hamilton got thrown out at first to end the quick 7th; the score was still tied 4-4.

Avery Smith started at-bat for Penn State, and he hit a single. Molinaro and Porter followed up with outs, though Smith got to second. Davis lined out to centerfield for another quick batting performance from Penn State.

Brown III continued his hot start, striking out Price looking. Larkins lined out to right field, but Pozzi hit a home run over left field. Brown III got back on pace with a pop-up out from Solomon to move to the final inning. Georgetown led 5-4.

The Hoyas started the last inning with a pitching change; O’Connor went out for right-handed pitcher Ethan Rucker. Rucker made quick work of the Nittany Lions batters. Jaconski and DeMucci both struck out swinging and Yaucher grounded out to third to end the game.

Up Next

Penn State returns home to face Iowa in its Big Ten opener on Friday at 5:30 p.m.

title: Penn State women's hockey captain Tessa Janecke named finalist for Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State senior forward Tessa Janecke has been named a top-three finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award.

In addition, she has also become the first player in program history to receive this honor. This award is presented annually to the top player in NCAA Division I women’s hockey.

TESSA JANECKE. HISTORY MAKER.Our captain becomes the FIRST Top-Three Finalist for the Patty Kaz in program history 🐐📰: https://t.co/53Ku8mPsyP#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/79QROSnq7s— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) March 11, 2026

Janecke recently returned to Happy Valley after representing Team USA at the 2026 Winter Olympics, where she helped the United States capture a gold medal in Milan. But her achievements didn't stop there as she became the first player in program history to be named one of the final three candidates for the prestigious award.

Janecke’s recognition comes after an outstanding season and career with the Nittany Lions. Penn State's captain is the program’s all-time leading scorer and has earned multiple honors, including Atlantic Hockey America Player of the Year and Forward of the Year.

The winner will be announced on March 21 during a ceremony at the HUB-Robeson Center in State College as part of the NCAA Women’s Frozen Four festivities.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Column | Who should Penn State men’s hockey start in net for the postseason?
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Guy Gadowsky faces a decision Wednesday evening: who starts in net for the Nittany Lions in the postseason?

Penn State has two very capable goaltenders in Josh Fleming and Kevin Reidler, and both have split netminding duties since the beginning of the 2025-26 campaign. However, one must take the reins ahead of the most important playoff stretch in program history.

Who should do it? It should be Fleming, and here’s why.

The rationale

Let’s start with the simple stuff.

Fleming leads the team with a 2.59 goals-allowed average, a .924 save percentage and has earned his club a point in 68.75% of his starts. Reidler, on the other hand, owns a 3.31 goals-against average, .901 save percentage and has earned his team a point in 61.11% of his starts.

Yet, despite Fleming leading Reidler in each category, he has started in two fewer games this season. Some may argue Gadowsky was keeping Fleming fresh by splitting time between the two goaltenders, but who starts in the postseason seems like a real decision he is mulling over.

To further Fleming’s case, he was named a Hobey Baker Award nominee and the National Co-Rookie of the Month for January. Additionally, his previously stated .924 save percentage ranks him 10th-best in the country. Reidler’s .901 save percentage puts him just outside the top 50.

Not to mention, in each of their four games against No. 1 Michigan and No. 3 Michigan State, the top-ranked and fifth-rated scoring offenses in the country, Fleming allowed a 3.50 goals-allowed average, while Reidler surrendered a 4.75.

In those contests, Fleming allowed five goals or more zero times, while Reidler hit the mark in three of the four games.

Now, after seeing Fleming has been superior in the basic statistics, let’s delve into the advanced metrics.

Fleming holds a .928 save percentage at even strength, an .898 save percentage shorthanded, a .924 save percentage on close opportunities and an .858 high-danger save percentage.

Reidler owns a .910 save percentage at even strength, an .887 shorthanded save percentage, a .907 save percentage on close opportunities and an .851 high-danger save percentage.

Yes, the trends continued, Fleming leads Reidler in all four major categories, and the numbers, while they seem it, aren’t all that close. And that’s not even the most eye-catching metric. The freshman has saved a whopping 13 goals above expected, while Reidler has stopped -0.2.

However, those are season-long statistics. So, what do the most recent ones say?

While it hasn’t been pretty for either goaltender in the Nittany Lions’ 2-6-2 rut over their last 10 contests, stretching back to the late January Beaver Stadium outdoor game, Fleming has outplayed Reidler over the past month.

In Fleming’s last three appearances, he has recorded a .917, .829 and .941 save percentage. Reidler, in his last three games, has posted an .875, .879 and .800 save percentage.

This is no knock on Reidler; he has been strong in net for Penn State this season. His nine games recording an .920 save percentage or higher, is more than Fleming’s seven and he’s played well outside of a few outlier games.

However, as Gadowsky noticed during the season when he moved Fleming to the Game 1 starting netminder, the Canadian has been the more consistent guy for the Nittany Lions between the pipes and gives the team a better chance to win.

Thus, Gadowsky should give Fleming the nod for the entirety of the postseason. Fleming should be fresh and he has shown he can stand toe-to-toe against the best goaltenders in the country, while stymying some of the most lethal offenses in the nation.
